doc.title: Zero-interest note — imputed interest
doc.event: On {issueDate}, Brant Co. signs a {face}, {termYears} zero-interest-bearing note when the market rate for similar borrowing is {marketRateAnnual}, receiving the note's present value in cash. Prepare the issuance entry and the first year-end entry.

variants.label: Face repaid at maturity; interest lives entirely in the discount

entries.caption: Issue the zero-interest note at its present value

lines.label: PV of face at 10%
lines.why: With no coupons, the issue price is just the PV of the face: 100,000 ÷ 1.10³ ≈ 75,131. That's all the cash that changes hands today.
lines.trap: Recording the note at face with 100,000 of cash would invent 24,869 the borrower never received.

lines.label: face − PV
lines.why: The entire future interest cost sits here on day one — a contra to Notes Payable that will amortize into expense at 10% per year.
lines.trap: 'Zero-interest' is a marketing term, not an accounting fact. The discount IS the interest, imputed at the market rate.

lines.label: face
lines.why: Face at maturity is the contract; the payable carries face, netted down by the discount for presentation.
lines.trap: Carrying value on day one = face − discount = the PV — not face.

lines.label: CV × market rate
lines.why: Same law as every liability in this chapter: carrying value × market rate. Year 1: 75,131 × 10% ≈ 7,513 — with zero cash paid.
lines.trap: No cash line! The stated rate is zero, so the ENTIRE expense amortizes the discount. Expense without cash is exactly what imputation means.

lines.label: = the full expense
lines.why: With cash at zero, amortization = expense − 0 = the whole expense. The carrying value climbs toward face: 75,131 → 82,644 → 90,909 → 100,000.
lines.trap: The discount amortization ACCELERATES each year (bigger CV × same rate) — a straight-line shortcut misstates every single year.

computationPaths.narration: A zero-interest note is a bond with a 0% coupon — the same schedule machine runs it: cash column all zeros, expense = CV × market rate, amortization = expense, CV compounds up to face. E13.17/18's 'imputation of interest' is this exact computation with the market rate estimated from comparable borrowings.

memorize.body: Zero-interest note: record at PV of face; each year expense = carrying value × imputed (market) rate; amortization = the full expense; CV compounds up to face by maturity.
Discount starts with D — it increases with a Debit, contra to Notes Payable. Here it's born at its maximum (face − PV) and dies to zero at maturity.

If the note came bundled with goods, services, or a below-market side deal (E13.18's 'note issued with a right'), the discount may be part interest, part something else — read what the PV gap is actually paying for.

questions.prompt: A $100,000, 3-year, zero-interest note is issued when the market rate is 10% (PV ≈ 75,131). What is year-1 interest expense?

distractors.feedback: Zero is the CASH paid, not the expense. The borrower's real cost is the discount unwinding at the market rate: 75,131 × 10%.
10% applies to the carrying value (75,131), not to face — face isn't owed-in-substance until the discount finishes amortizing.

traces.label: Face − PV ↔ the discount ↔ three years of expense

build.scaffold: First find the PV — that's the only real number on day one. Then every year is two lines: expense (CV × rate) and discount (same amount). No cash moves until maturity.
